title: A2QTGN: Adaptive Amplitude Quantum-Integrated Temporal Graph Network for Dynamic Link Prediction
authors: Nouhaila Innan, M. Murali Karthick, Simeon Kandan Sonar, Vivek Chaturvedi, Muhammad Shafique
abstract: Dynamic link prediction is important for modeling evolving interactions in complex systems, including social, communication, financial, and transportation networks. Classical temporal graph models capture sequential dependencies, but they may struggle to represent concurrent and rapidly changing node-edge interactions in large dynamic graphs. We propose A2QTGN (Adaptive Amplitude Quantum-Integrated Temporal Graph Network), a hybrid quantum-classical framework that combines adaptive amplitude encoding with a Temporal Graph Network backbone. The proposed mechanism represents node interaction features as quantum states and selectively refreshes amplitude embeddings based on temporal activity, preserving stable node states while emphasizing meaningful structural changes. This design reduces unnecessary quantum re-encoding and improves temporal representation for link prediction. Experiments on five Temporal Graph Benchmark datasets show that A2QTGN achieves strong predictive and ranking performance across diverse dynamic graphs. Ablation studies confirm the importance of both the quantum embedding module and the adaptive update strategy, while hardware-aware inference using a noisy backend and limited real-device execution supports the feasibility of near-term quantum-assisted temporal graph learning.
summary: This paper introduces a hybrid quantum-classical framework for predicting future interactions in evolving networks. By using an adaptive mechanism to only update quantum embeddings when significant changes occur, it reduces computational overhead and improves prediction accuracy. The model is shown to be effective even under the noisy conditions of near-term quantum hardware.
main_problem: The challenge of performing efficient dynamic link prediction in evolving networks by overcoming the limitations of classical temporal graph models and the high computational cost of re-encoding quantum states at every timestep.
main_result: The A2QTGN framework achieves superior predictive performance across multiple temporal graph benchmarks and demonstrates robustness to noise and limited shot counts on real IBM quantum hardware.
method: A hybrid quantum-classical approach using Adaptive Amplitude Encoding (AAE) to selectively update quantum embeddings only when temporal feature variations exceed a threshold, integrated with a Temporal Graph Network (TGN) backbone.
model_or_system: A hybrid quantum-classical neural network architecture utilizing PennyLane for quantum circuit execution, specifically employing amplitude encoding on 8 qubits and evaluating performance on IBM quantum devices (e.g., ibm_torino).
key_observables: Expectation values of Pauli-Z operators, prediction accuracy, precision, AUC, and Mean Reciprocal Rank (MRR).
important_parameters: Activity factor (alpha), update threshold (tau), number of qubits (8), and measurement shot counts (100-shot vs 2048-shot).
main_assumptions: The model assumes that temporal feature variations can be effectively captured by a threshold-based re-encoding strategy and that the quantum-classical interface can be optimized via gradient-based learning.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the concept of dynamic graphs; Figure 2 details the A2MTGN pipeline including the adaptive update loop; Figure 3 displays performance convergence curves; and various tables present ablation studies and hardware-aware performance metrics.
paper_structure: The paper identifies limitations in classical and static quantum graph models, proposes the A2QTGN architecture and its adaptive mechanism, details the experimental setup on various benchmarks, presents ablation studies on encoding and update strategies, and concludes with hardware-aware performance evaluations.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 2: Pipeline of the A2QTGN methodology. The system processes a temporal interaction stream {(ut, vt, Xt, Ft, t)} and converts it into future link predictions through four coordinated modules: (a) temporal interaction data forming the discrete-time graph sequence Gt = (Vt, Et, Xt, Ft); (b) the AAE, which computes temporal feature variation ∆xi,t, derives the activity factor αi,t = σ(β∥∆xi,t∥2), and conditionally re-encodes the node into a quantum state |ψi,t⟩whose expectation values yield the embedding zi,t; (c) the TGN memory mechanism, where node memories mi,t are updated using the fused representation hi,t = NN([mprev i,t ∥zi,t]); and (d) the link prediction module, which scores...
Fig. 3: Performance convergence on various datasets.
